Nineteenth Century

Charles Sanders Peirce

1839 – 1914

Pragmatism, semiotics, habit, and a mind whose structure is continuous with the cosmos.

I will assume “Pierce” means Charles Sanders Peirce—usually spelled Peirce—the American pragmatist, logician, semiotician, and metaphysician. Peirce did not leave a single book called Philosophy of Mind, but his writings on cognition, signs, logic, psychology, perception, habit, personality, synechism, objective idealism, and immortality together yield one of the most original philosophies of mind in the modern period.

The shortest accurate formulation is this: Peirce’s philosophy of mind is an anti-Cartesian, semiotic, inferential, habit-based, temporally extended, socially distributed, evolutionary, synechistic, psychophysical monism. Mind is not, for him, a private inner substance inspecting its own ideas. Mind is a living process of signs, feelings, habits, inferences, interpretations, and self-correcting conduct. It is continuous with nature, body, community, language, and cosmic evolution. His mature metaphysics is often described as a kind of objective idealism or qualified panpsychistic psychophysical monism: matter is not the opposite of mind but mind hardened into habit. Peirce is therefore neither a Cartesian dualist, nor a reductive materialist, nor a merely subjective idealist. He is trying to dissolve the mind-body problem by rejecting the sharp ontological cut that generated it.

1. The basic orientation: anti-Cartesian psychology

Peirce’s philosophy of mind begins with a rejection of the Cartesian picture. The Cartesian model treats the mind as a private, immediately self-present inner domain: I know my own thoughts directly; I infer the outer world only indirectly; the self is given before public signs, other persons, or scientific inquiry. Peirce reverses almost every part of this model. He denies that we possess an infallible faculty of intuition, denies that self-consciousness is primitive, denies introspection as a direct route to a private internal world, denies thought without signs, and denies the meaningfulness of the absolutely incognizable. In his 1868 “Questions Concerning Certain Faculties Claimed for Man,” he argues that there is no need to posit an intuitive self-consciousness; the child’s sense of self arises through ignorance, error, resistance, and social correction.

This does not mean that Peirce denies consciousness, feeling, sensation, or inward life. Rather, he denies that the mind is known by a privileged, self-authenticating inner gaze. Psychological knowledge must be inferential, fallible, and publicly corrigible. In a crucial passage, he says that the only way to investigate psychological questions is by inference from external facts. This makes Peirce one of the great anti-introspectionists before twentieth-century behaviorism, but his position is not behaviorism, because he does not eliminate feeling, consciousness, or mind. He reconceives them as phenomena that become intelligible through signs, habits, conduct, and continuity rather than through private inspection.

The anti-Cartesian core is also anti-foundationalist. Peirce rejects the idea that cognition begins from isolated, indubitable mental atoms. No cognition is first in the Cartesian sense; every cognition is determined by previous cognition, and any meaningful thought must be interpretable by further thought. The mind is therefore not a sequence of self-contained mental instants. It is a temporal continuum of interpretive transitions.

2. Thought as sign: Peirce’s semiotic theory of mind

Peirce’s most important thesis for philosophy of mind is that all thought is in signs. A thought is not an inner mental item that later receives a sign for communication. Thought is already semiotic. It stands for something, in some respect, to some further interpretant. Peirce’s basic sign relation is triadic: a sign, or representamen, represents an object to an interpretant; that interpretant can itself become a further sign, generating an open-ended chain of interpretation.

This has several consequences. First, mental content is not dyadic. It is not merely a relation between a subject and an object. It is triadic: object, sign, interpretant. Second, meaning is not exhausted by an instantaneous mental occurrence. Meaning lies in the possible future development of interpretation. Third, the mind is intrinsically temporal: a thought only functions as thought insofar as it can determine another thought. Fourth, because signs are public, shareable, and embodied in language, gesture, diagrams, experiments, habits, and institutions, mind is never purely private.

This is why Peirce’s theory of mind is also a theory of language and community. In “Some Consequences of Four Incapacities,” Peirce famously argues that the person is not an isolated ego behind signs. The human being is itself a sign-process; “the man is the thought.” He even says that a person’s language is, in a deep sense, the sum of the person, because thought lives in signs that continue beyond the present individual moment.

The modern philosophy of mind often treats intentionality—the aboutness or representational directedness of mental states—as a central problem. Brentano is usually credited with reintroducing intentionality into modern philosophy of mind in the late nineteenth century. Peirce belongs in the same historical region, but his approach is different: intentionality is not primarily an inner act of consciousness directed at an object; it is a triadic semiotic relation embedded in inferential, communicative, and practical life.

3. Mind, inference, and the rejection of psychological immediacy

Peirce repeatedly treats mental action as inferential. In early writings, he even says that every exercise of mind consists in inference. This must be interpreted carefully. He does not mean that all mental life is explicit syllogistic reasoning. He means that cognition has a mediated structure: one state of mind determines another under a rule-like or sign-like relation. Sensation, emotion, attention, association, belief, and judgment are not sealed atoms; they are transitions in a larger process.

This inferentialism gives Peirce a distinctive view of perception. A raw percept, in his later terminology, is not itself a proposition and cannot serve as a premise. It is forced upon us; it has Secondness, the brute character of encounter, resistance, and compulsion. But what we ordinarily call perception is already a perceptual judgment: “that appears yellow,” “this is a chair,” and so forth. Such judgments represent the percept, not by copying it, but by functioning indexically—as symptoms or signs compelled by the percept. Thus perception is neither Cartesian intuition nor mere subjective construction. It is a semiotic response to brute experiential constraint.

This is a powerful alternative to both empiricism and rationalism. Against empiricism, Peirce denies that knowledge begins with simple sense-data that are immediately known. Against rationalism, he denies that cognition rests on innate, transparent intellectual intuitions. Perception is fallible, compelled, semiotic, and inferentially usable. It is not a foundation in the Cartesian sense; it is an entry-point into inquiry.

4. Belief, doubt, habit, and action

Peirce’s pragmatism is also a philosophy of mind. In “How to Make Our Ideas Clear,” he treats belief not as a private mental glow, but as a rule or habit of action. Doubt is an irritation that disrupts settled conduct; inquiry aims to replace doubt with belief; belief establishes a habit that guides future behavior. Differences in belief are differences in possible conduct, broadly understood.

This is not crude behavioral reduction. Peirce is not saying that a belief is just a bodily movement. He is saying that the meaning of a belief lies in the conceivable practical effects and habits of conduct that would follow from it. A belief’s content is dispositional, future-oriented, and inferential. To believe something is to be prepared to act, reason, expect, perceive, and interpret in certain ways under relevant circumstances.

Habit is therefore central. At the psychological level, habits are acquired dispositions of response, association, expectation, and interpretation. At the logical level, habits are rules of inference and conduct. At the metaphysical level, habits are the way regularity itself develops in the universe. Peirce’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from this general theory of habit-taking. Mind is not a static substance; it is a habit-forming, habit-modifying, self-interpreting process.

5. Consciousness, feeling, and the categories

Peirce’s account of mind must not be confused with a purely intellectualist theory. He gives a fundamental place to feeling. His universal categories—Firstness, Secondness, and Thirdness—are indispensable here. Firstness is quality, possibility, immediacy, suchness, feeling. Secondness is actuality, reaction, resistance, brute encounter. Thirdness is mediation, law, habit, representation, generality. Mind includes all three: felt quality, encounter with otherness, and interpretive mediation.

In his writings on forms of consciousness, Peirce distinguishes feeling, altersense, and medisense. Feeling is the immediate qualitative content of consciousness. Altersense is the consciousness of a second, an other that resists or compels us; sensation and will belong here. Medisense is consciousness of mediation, process, bringing-to-mind; abstraction, suggestion, and association belong here. This gives Peirce a phenomenology of mind that is both richer than behaviorism and less private than Cartesian introspection.

Peirce also sharply distinguishes mind from consciousness. He criticizes psychologists who identify mind with consciousness, partly because association, habit-taking, and unconscious mental processes would then fall outside psychology, even though they are central to it. He accepts the existence of unconscious mind and says that feeling is the inward aspect of things, while mind is essentially external. This last claim is one of his most radical: mind is not exhausted by what appears in focal consciousness; it lives in signs, habits, relations, tendencies, and interpretive structures.

His experimental and psychological remarks reinforce this. Peirce describes consciousness as something like a deep field in which many ideas operate below the upper layer of reflex awareness. Ideas can influence thought before they become objects of attention. This anticipates later concerns with unconscious cognition, implicit discrimination, background processing, and non-focal awareness, though Peirce embeds these in a semiotic and metaphysical framework rather than in a purely neurological one.

6. Empirical psychology: Peirce the experimentalist

Peirce was not merely a speculative philosopher of mind. With Joseph Jastrow, he conducted important psychophysical work on small differences of sensation. Their 1885 paper tested whether there is a fixed least perceptible difference in sensation. They used pressure discrimination experiments, recorded degrees of confidence, used screens to prevent visual cues, and randomized sequences with shuffled cards.

The philosophical importance of these experiments is considerable. Peirce and Jastrow argued that subjects could make discriminations even when they reported no conscious confidence in doing so. Their results suggested that faint sensations below explicit awareness can still guide judgment. This is exactly the kind of result one would expect from Peirce’s broader theory: cognition is not limited to focal introspection; signs, discriminations, and tendencies operate below reflective self-consciousness.

Peirce’s empirical psychology is thus continuous with his anti-Cartesian epistemology. If introspection is unreliable and incomplete, psychology must become experimental, statistical, public, and inferential. Yet Peirce also resists reducing logic to psychology. Logic, for him, is normative and semiotic; psychology is a special science of mental phenomena. Psychology can study how we in fact think, associate, attend, and form habits, but it cannot determine the validity of inference merely by reporting psychological regularities. His classification of the sciences places psychology among the special or “psychical” sciences, while logic belongs to normative science and, in its broadest sense, to semeiotic.

7. Time, continuity, and the law of mind

Peirce’s mind is temporally extended. Consciousness does not exist as a knife-edge instant. In “The Law of Mind,” he argues that ideas spread continuously, affect other ideas, lose intensity, gain generality, and become welded with other ideas. Mental life is not a string of discrete atomic states but a continuum of feeling and interpretation.

This temporalism is crucial for personal identity. A person is not wholly present at any instant. Personality is a coordination of ideas, a general living continuity, a developmental tendency. Peirce explicitly says personality must be lived in time and cannot be apprehended in an instant. It includes reference to the future: character is not merely what one has done, but a living general idea that shapes possible future conduct.

Here Peirce anticipates process theories of mind and personhood. The person is not a mental substance plus accidental experiences. The person is a temporally extended semiotic-habitual process. Identity is continuity of interpretive tendency, character, memory, action, social embodiment, and future-directed habit—not the bare sameness of an inner ego.

8. Selfhood and social mind

Peirce’s theory of the self is deeply social. Since thought is in signs, and signs are essentially communicable, the self is not prior to language, community, and interpretation. The individual self emerges within a field of signs, errors, corrections, expectations, and shared practices. A purely private self, cut off from others and the future, is for Peirce an abstraction bordering on illusion.

His synechism radicalizes this. Peirce rejects the idea that one is absolutely oneself and not at all another. Neighbors, analogues, communities, and future interpreters are continuous with the individual self in varying degrees. This does not erase individuality; Peirce is not saying persons are literally indistinguishable. He is saying that selfhood is not an indivisible atom. It is a continuity of relations.

Peirce even considers the possibility of group or corporate personality. In “Man’s Glassy Essence,” he suggests that persons require closely connected feelings and that forms of collective consciousness—esprit de corps, national feeling, religious community—should not be dismissed as mere metaphors. This gives him a proto-theory of distributed or collective mind, though framed in nineteenth-century metaphysical language.

9. Mind and body: synechistic objective idealism

Peirce’s mature answer to the mind-body problem is synechistic psychophysical monism. Synechism is his doctrine of continuity. It opposes sharp metaphysical cuts: between mind and body, phenomenon and substrate, individual and community, life and matter, waking and sleeping, even being and non-being. He explicitly says synechism cannot abide dualism in the sense of dividing reality into unrelated chunks. Physical and psychical phenomena are not entirely distinct kinds of substance; they differ by degree, some being more mental and spontaneous, others more material and regular.

The result is objective idealism, but not subjective idealism. Peirce does not say that the world is my idea, or that reality depends on individual consciousness. Rather, matter itself is mind under the dominance of habit. In one of his most compressed formulations, matter is “mind hide-bound with habits.” The Stanford Encyclopedia summarizes this as Peirce’s view that matter is congealed or effete mind and that mind pervades nature in varying degrees.

This makes Peirce a qualified panpsychist or panexperientialist, though the label needs care. He does not claim that stones deliberate or that electrons have human-like consciousness. His claim is that spontaneity, feeling, and mind-like continuity are not late metaphysical miracles injected into dead matter. Rather, matter is the stabilized, regularized, habitual form of a more general mental or quasi-mental process. The physical is not non-mental substance; it is mind become regular, inertial, and law-governed.

This is tied to his cosmology. Peirce’s tychism says objective chance or spontaneity is real. His evolutionary cosmology says laws are not eternal fixed necessities but habits acquired by the universe. His agapism adds that growth occurs not merely by mechanical necessity or brute competition, but through continuity, sympathy, and creative generalization. Mind, habit, law, and evolution belong to the same metaphysical story.

10. Soul, immortality, incorporeal mind, and spiritual consciousness

Peirce does discuss immortality, soul, spiritual consciousness, and religious implications, but he does not defend a standard Cartesian doctrine of an immaterial soul-substance separable from the body. His position is subtler and more continuous with his semiotic metaphysics.

First, he rejects mind-body dualism. A Peircean soul cannot be a second substance radically detached from matter, because synechism denies sharp physical/psychical separation. If there is immortality, it must be understood through continuity, signhood, social embodiment, and spiritual generality—not through a private ego-substance leaving the body intact.

Second, Peirce sometimes interprets personal survival semiotically. In early “Consciousness and Language” materials, he suggests that a person’s immortality depends on the person’s being a true symbol. A person’s character or idiosyncratic way of regarding things is the “idea” of that person; if that idea is true, it has a kind of enduring reality. This is not ordinary biological survival, but neither is it mere memory. It is survival as an objective, living, interpretable general meaning.

Third, in “Immortality in the Light of Synechism,” Peirce applies continuity directly to death. He says synechism refuses to believe that carnal consciousness ceases quickly at death, while also admitting the lack of observational data. More importantly, he distinguishes carnal consciousness from social consciousness and spiritual consciousness. A person’s spirit is embodied in others through social consciousness, and spiritual consciousness is connected with the person’s being embodied in the universe as an archetypal or eternal verity.

Fourth, Peirce leaves room for spiritual influences and even says his synechistic doctrine presents no philosophical obstacle to them. In “The Law of Mind,” he also says evolutionary philosophy is not hostile to the idea of a personal creator. But this should not be read as a conventional proof of ghosts, disembodied egos, or a Cartesian afterlife. His view is that mind-to-mind communication, personality, God, and spiritual life must be interpreted through continuity, generality, signs, and living ideas.

A fair reconstruction is therefore: Peirce does not offer a substance-dualist doctrine of the soul; he offers a synechistic-semiotic doctrine of personal, social, and spiritual continuity. Carnal consciousness is embodied and finite; personal meaning continues in signs, communities, and habits; spiritual consciousness may be a deeper continuity that ordinary bodily life obscures. He is speculative here, and he acknowledges the evidential difficulty, but these comments are not peripheral. They follow from his metaphysics of continuity.

11. Historical position in the development of philosophy of mind

Historically, Peirce stands at a crossroads. Classical and modern philosophy of mind often centered on dualism: whether mind and body are fundamentally different kinds of things, and how they interact. Descartes made the mind-body problem acute by treating mind and body as distinct substances. Peirce’s strategy is not to solve Cartesian dualism from within, but to undercut its premises: no private intuitive self, no incorrigible introspection, no thought without signs, no absolute gulf between mental and physical.

Relative to nineteenth-century psychology, Peirce is both a participant and a critic. Like William James, he belongs to the generation in which psychology, physiology, philosophy, and scientific method were still deeply intertwined. James’s Principles of Psychology famously developed ideas such as the stream of thought; Peirce instead gives a more logical, semiotic, and inferential account of the stream-like continuity of mind.

Relative to Brentano and later phenomenology, Peirce offers an alternative theory of intentionality. Brentano’s intentionality is typically framed as the directedness of mental acts; Peirce’s intentionality is sign-mediated, triadic, public, and not confined to conscious acts. This makes Peirce a crucial but often under-integrated figure in the history of intentionality, representation, and philosophy of language.

Relative to behaviorism, Peirce is an ancestor but not a member. He agrees that psychological hypotheses require public, behavioral, experimental, and inferential constraint. But he rejects the exclusion of feeling and consciousness from philosophy. He also embeds habit and behavior in a richer semiotic metaphysics. So he anticipates parts of functionalism and pragmatist psychology without reducing mind to observable motion.

Relative to contemporary philosophy of mind, Peirce anticipates several later developments: externalism, inferentialism, embodied cognition, social theories of mind, process metaphysics, panpsychism, and distributed cognition. These are affinities, not identities. Peirce’s framework is more metaphysical and semiotic than most analytic externalism, more realist than linguistic conventionalism, more anti-reductionist than functionalism, and more scientifically minded than much speculative idealism.

12. A systematic Peircean philosophy of mind

If one extracts a comprehensive philosophy of mind from Peirce, it can be stated as the following set of theses.

First, mind is semiotic. To think is to be in signs. Mental content is not a private object but a sign-relation that connects object, sign, and interpretant.

Second, mind is inferential. Cognition is mediated by transitions from sign to sign, belief to consequence, percept to judgment, doubt to inquiry, habit to action.

Third, mind is temporal. No thought has its full meaning in an instant. A thought’s identity lies in its possible development into future interpretants.

Fourth, mind is habitual. Beliefs are habits of action; meanings are conceivable practical effects; persons are organized systems of habits; matter itself is habit hardened.

Fifth, mind is affective and qualitative. Feeling is real and fundamental. Peirce is not an eliminativist; Firstness gives qualitative consciousness a basic place.

Sixth, mind is reactive and embodied. Sensation and will involve Secondness: resistance, compulsion, effort, encounter with otherness.

Seventh, mind is interpretive and generalizing. Thirdness—law, mediation, habit, representation—is the structure that makes cognition, meaning, and rationality possible.

Eighth, mind is not identical with focal consciousness. Much mental life operates below reflective awareness: association, discrimination, habit-taking, suggestion, and implicit inference.

Ninth, mind is social. The self is constituted through signs, language, correction, communication, and community. Individual consciousness is only one locus of mind.

Tenth, mind is continuous with nature. Peirce rejects the absolute separation of physical and psychical. Matter is mind made regular; mind is nature in its more spontaneous, feeling, sign-generating aspect.

Eleventh, personhood is a living general idea. A person is not a mental atom but a temporally extended, future-oriented, socially embedded, self-interpreting pattern of conduct and meaning.

Twelfth, soul and immortality must be semiotic and synechistic if they are to be philosophically defensible. Peirce does not give us a Cartesian soul; he gives us personal continuity through signhood, social consciousness, spiritual generality, and the possible persistence of living ideas.

13. Strengths and unresolved tensions

Peirce’s philosophy of mind has major strengths. It avoids the myth of the private inner theater. It explains intentionality through signs rather than mysterious mental pointing. It integrates belief, meaning, action, and inquiry. It treats consciousness as real without making it epistemically infallible. It gives a serious account of unconscious cognition and habit. It makes sociality constitutive rather than accidental. And it offers a non-reductive alternative to both dualism and materialism.

The tensions are equally important. His claim that all mental action is inferential can sound too intellectualist unless balanced by his category of Firstness and his theory of percepts. His objective idealism and panpsychistic language remain speculative, and his biological discussions of protoplasm belong to nineteenth-century science. His treatment of immortality is suggestive rather than demonstrative, and he admits the lack of observational data. Finally, his tendency to connect logic, psychology, metaphysics, theology, and cosmology can make it difficult to determine when he is offering empirical psychology, normative logic, phenomenology, or speculative metaphysics.

The best final judgment is that Peirce offers one of the most ambitious anti-Cartesian philosophies of mind ever developed. Mind is not a ghost in a machine, not a brain-state alone, not a bundle of private impressions, and not a mere pattern of behavior. Mind is semiosis becoming conscious, habitual, social, embodied, and self-correcting within an evolving universe of signs.
